content: I want to understand 3D Gaussian Splatting end to end. Start with the Kerbl 2023 paper.

reasoning: Pull the canonical 3DGS page, then walk the dependency graph backward to NeRF/MVS prerequisites and forward to follow-ons.
content: I'll pull the canonical 3DGS paper, then trace its prerequisites and direct successors via the graph.

content.title: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering
content.body: Kerbl et al. introduce 3D GaussianSplatting, a primitive-based representation for novel-view synthesis that recovers a scene as a collection of anisotropic 3D Gaussians and renders them by differentiable rasterisation. Each Gaussian carries a position, a full 3x3 covariance, an opacity, and a view-dependent colour encoded as spherical harmonics. The optimisation is seeded from the sparse PointCloud produced by a standard StructureFromMotion pass; an interleaved densification and pruning schedule clones, splits, and removes primitives during training; a fast tile rasteriser keeps the backward pass closed-form. >=30 FPS at 1080p on unbounded scenes.

reasoning: Two directions: backward to NeRF/SfM, forward through SuGaR/2DGS/Scaffold-GS/MonoGS/4DGS/Revising. Read NeRF first to establish the prerequisite.
content: Clean dependency graph. NeRF is the only upstream paper edge. Let me read it next.

content.title: NeRF: Representing Scenes as Neural Radiance Fields for View Synthesis
content.body: Mildenhall et al. introduce Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF): a 5D coordinate-MLP that maps (x, y, z, theta, phi) to a volume density and view-dependent radiance, with novel views synthesised by querying along camera rays and compositing via classical volume rendering. Positional encoding and a hierarchical coarse-to-fine sampling scheme make it work. Per-scene, photometric loss only, no explicit geometry supervision.

content.title: SuGaR: Surface-Aligned Gaussian Splatting
content.body: Guedon and Lepetit close 3DGS's mesh-extraction gap with a regulariser that aligns Gaussians to the underlying scene surface, then runs Poisson reconstruction on the oriented centres to produce a watertight mesh in minutes. An optional refinement stage binds Gaussians to the mesh by barycentric coordinate so the mesh provides edit handles.

content.title: 2D Gaussian Splatting
content.body: Huang et al. collapse each volumetric primitive to a flat oriented disk so the implied geometry is view-consistent. Perspective-correct rasteriser; depth-distortion and normal-consistency regularisers.

content.title: Gaussian Splatting SLAM
content.body: Matsuki et al. (MonoGS) use 3DGS as the sole map representation in a monocular SLAM loop. Camera tracking by differentiating the splat render w.r.t. pose. About 3 FPS. Handles RGB-D when available without architectural change.

content.title: 4D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Dynamic Scene Rendering
content.body: Wu et al. extend 3DGS to dynamic scenes by attaching a deformation field (HexPlane-style spatio-temporal feature) to each canonical Gaussian. Renders dynamic scenes in real time; struggles past short clips.

content.title: Mathematical Supplement for the gsplat Library
content.body: Ye et al. derive the closed-form forward and backward passes of the tile-based splatting rasteriser used by the gsplat reference implementation (nerfstudio-project/gsplat). The supplement is the canonical reference for the gradient of the rendered colour with respect to each Gaussian's mean, covariance, opacity, and spherical-harmonic coefficients.

content.title: Scaffold-GS: Structured 3D Gaussians for View-Adaptive Rendering
content.body: Lu et al. replace the free Gaussian soup with anchor points on a regular grid; each anchor spawns a small set of view-dependent offsets and attributes via a tiny MLP. Lower primitive count, better low-frequency regions.

reasoning: Eight papers in hand plus NeRF prerequisite. Lay them out as a dependency tree: NeRF -> 3DGS -> {SuGaR, 2DGS, Scaffold-GS, MonoGS, 4DGS, Revising, gsplat-impl}.
content:
Here is the end-to-end lineage:

  Prerequisite
    NeRF (Mildenhall 2020, arxiv-2003-08934)
        - establishes the differentiable volume-rendering paradigm and per-scene photometric optimisation that 3DGS replaces with primitives.

  Anchor
    3D Gaussian Splatting (Kerbl 2023, arxiv-2308-04079)
        - swaps NeRF's ray-marched MLP for a tile-rasterised cloud of anisotropic 3D Gaussians seeded from SfM. >=30 FPS at 1080p.

  Implementation reference
    gsplat math supplement (Ye 2023, arxiv-2312-02121)
        - derives the closed-form forward / backward of the tile rasteriser; canonical reference for any GS gradient question.

  Geometry branch
    SuGaR (Guedon 2023, arxiv-2311-12775) -> mesh from a regularised Gaussian field.
    2D Gaussian Splatting (Huang 2024, arxiv-2403-17888) -> view-consistent geometry by construction.

  Structure branch
    Scaffold-GS (Lu 2023, arxiv-2312-00109) -> anchor-based replacement for the free soup.
    Revising Densification (Bulo 2024, arxiv-2404-06109) -> corrects the clone/split/prune schedule downstream methods inherit.

  SLAM branch
    Gaussian Splatting SLAM / MonoGS (Matsuki 2023, arxiv-2312-06741) -> 3DGS as the sole map representation under live monocular capture.

  Dynamic branch
    4D Gaussian Splatting (Wu 2023, arxiv-2310-08528) -> deformation field attached to each canonical Gaussian.

Read in that order, the original paper plus the seven downstream nodes cover the representation, its math, its surface story, its structure story, its SLAM story, and its dynamic story. If you want code to run alongside the reading, the `graphdeco-inria/gaussian-splatting` repo is the reference and `nerfstudio-project/gsplat` is the cleaner library implementation.

Walk me through adaptive density control properly. The Kerbl paper just says 'gradient-based split and clone' — what's actually in the gradient they threshold on? Position only, or the full parameter set?

reasoning: The original 3DGS paper thresholds on the per-Gaussian view-space position-gradient magnitude averaged over the last N iterations. Above the threshold and small primitive: clone. Above threshold and large primitive: split. The gsplat math supplement has the exact backward derivation — let me grab it to be precise rather than from memory.
content: Position-gradient only, but it's the gradient of the photometric loss w.r.t. the 2D screen-space projected mean — not 3D position directly. The gsplat supplement has the clean derivation; let me pull it.

content.title: Mathematical Supplement for the gsplat Library
content.body: Ye et al. derive the closed-form forward and backward passes of the tile-based splatting rasteriser used by the gsplat reference implementation. For each Gaussian i, the backward pass returns gradients of the rendered colour with respect to the 3D mean mu_i, the covariance Sigma_i (parameterised via scale + rotation), the opacity alpha_i, and the SH coefficients f_i. Adaptive density control in Kerbl et al. thresholds on the per-Gaussian L2 norm of the *screen-space* gradient of the mean averaged over the last 100 iterations. Cloning duplicates a primitive and shrinks both copies; splitting replaces a too-large primitive with two smaller ones aligned with the dominant scale axis.

arguments.query: adaptive density control clone split threshold

facts.subject: Paper:arxiv-2308-04079
facts.object: screen-space position-gradient L2 norm averaged over last 100 iters

facts.subject: Paper:arxiv-2404-06109
facts.object: criterion replaced by per-primitive variance of position gradients across views
facts.evidence: Bulo et al. argue the mean-magnitude criterion under-densifies in regions with high-variance multi-view disagreement.

reasoning: Good. The exact mechanism is: per-Gaussian, take the gradient of the rendered colour loss w.r.t. the 2D projected mean (post-projection, screen-space), L2-norm it, average over the last 100 iterations. If that scalar exceeds tau_pos AND the primitive's max scale axis is below tau_scale, clone (duplicate and shrink). If it exceeds tau_pos AND the scale exceeds tau_scale, split (replace with two smaller children). Bulo's revision changes the *gradient summary statistic* from mean-magnitude to variance-across-views.
content:
Here's what's actually in the criterion:

  - Forward pass renders the tile-based splatter; backward pass returns dL/d(mu_i) where mu_i is the *screen-space* 2D projected mean of Gaussian i (not the 3D mean directly).
  - Per Gaussian, the L2 norm of that screen-space gradient is accumulated and averaged over the last 100 training iterations. One scalar per primitive.
  - Decision rule (Kerbl Section 5.2):
      * scalar > tau_pos AND max_scale_axis < tau_scale -> CLONE. Duplicate the primitive in-place, shrink each child along the chosen scale axis. Intuition: 'this region is under-reconstructed but the primitive isn't too big — add coverage.'
      * scalar > tau_pos AND max_scale_axis >= tau_scale -> SPLIT. Replace with two smaller primitives along the dominant axis. Intuition: 'this region is under-reconstructed AND the primitive is too big — refine resolution.'
      * scalar <= tau_pos -> no action.
  - Pruning is independent: opacity < tau_alpha or excessive size triggers removal.
  - The pathology Bulo (arxiv-2404-06109) attacks: averaging the L2 norm hides multi-view disagreement. A Gaussian that's perfect for one camera but wrong for another gets a low mean magnitude (cancellation) and is never split, even though it should be. The Bulo replacement is the per-view *variance* of the same gradient instead of its mean.
OK. And how does that interact with the SH color encoding when a Gaussian is cloned? The 3-channel coefficients up to some degree — what happens to them on duplication?

reasoning: On clone, both children inherit the parent's full SH coefficient vector verbatim — Kerbl's recipe is the cheapest valid initialisation. The subsequent gradient steps differentiate them. The gsplat supplement spells out the SH gradient backward pass; let me re-check whether there's an explicit clone-time SH handling rule.
content: Both children inherit the parent's SH coefficients verbatim at clone time. Let me confirm against the gsplat supplement's backward pass.

content.body: ...The SH coefficients f_i parameterise view-dependent radiance per Gaussian; the rasteriser evaluates the SH basis at the view direction (d_x, d_y, d_z) per pixel and weights by tile blending. Backward returns df_i for each channel and each basis index, propagated through the alpha-composited tile aggregate. Densification (clone/split) is treated as a parameter-space operation that copies the existing SH vector unchanged; subsequent optimisation differentiates the children naturally because their projected screen positions (and therefore their per-pixel contribution gradients) diverge after the first step.

reasoning: Confirmed. SH coefficients are copied unchanged on clone. The interaction with the screen-space-gradient criterion is subtle: both children start with identical SH but their screen-space projections diverge immediately if their 3D means are perturbed (Kerbl perturbs each child along the dominant scale axis by a small fraction of the scale). After the next forward pass each child sees a slightly different rendered colour at its pixel set, so dL/df_i diverges, and the SH vectors decorrelate within a few optimisation steps. This is also why the original densification under-densifies view-inconsistent regions: identical SH on both children means the cloned pair *initially* renders the same view-dependent colour everywhere, so the first-step gradient that should diverge them in colour space is suppressed.
content:
Concretely:

  - At clone time both children inherit the full SH vector (degree-d, 3 channels, (d+1)^2 coefficients per channel — so 48 floats for degree 3) verbatim. No re-init, no perturbation in colour space.
  - The only perturbation is positional: Kerbl shifts each child's 3D mean along the dominant scale axis by a small fraction of the parent's scale, and halves the scale on that axis. The projected screen positions of the two children therefore diverge by one or two pixels after re-projection.
  - From the next forward pass onward, the two children contribute to slightly different pixels' rendered colour. Their backward gradients df_i diverge, and within ~50-100 steps the SH vectors have decorrelated.
  - Subtle interaction with the densification criterion: because both children start with the *same* SH vector, the very first post-clone step has nearly identical view-dependent radiance from both, so the immediate dL/d(mu_i) gradient that distinguishes them is dominated by purely positional disagreement. View-inconsistent radiance disagreement (the exact failure mode Bulo's variance criterion targets) only contributes to the gradient after the SH coefficients have decorrelated. The original criterion measures the *mean* gradient over 100 iterations — long enough for the SH to decorrelate, but the cancellation in early steps still washes out the signal Bulo wants to pick up.

That's the mechanical reason Bulo's variance-of-gradient criterion fires more aggressively than mean-magnitude on the same scene: it never averages over the early-window cancellation.

Lineage tree stands: NeRF -> 3DGS -> {SuGaR, 2DGS, Scaffold-GS, MonoGS, 4DGS, Revising Densification, gsplat math supplement}. The mechanics drill-down ties the Bulo correction back to the original criterion via the SH-decorrelation latency in the early post-clone window — useful to know if you ever need to re-tune the densification thresholds or compare against the revised schedule.
